# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 89

This is turn 89. Turns 1 through 88 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 89.

# Turn 89 — standalone fixed-frame proof note

The candidate remains

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

The tame relation \(\tau^\sigma=\tau^2\) is implicit. In the mathematical argument, every displayed exponent \(40491355905\) denotes the genuine profinite idempotent
\[
\omega _2\in\widehat{\mathbf Z},
\]
with \(2\)-component \(1\) and all odd components \(0\); the integer is only its required residue modulo \(M=85667662080\). 

Let \(\Gamma_A\) denote the resulting four-generated profinite marked object, with
\[
W_A=\overline{\langle\!\langle x_0,x_1\rangle\!\rangle}
\]
pro-\(2\). Its finite epimorphisms are exactly the generating verifier tuples: those satisfying the two relations and with \(x_0,x_1\in O_2(G)\).

## Fixed-tame-frame theorem

Let \(G\) be finite, let \(L\triangleleft G\) be a normal \(2\)-subgroup, put \(H=G/L\), and fix a tame epimorphism
\[
\alpha:T_{\mathrm{tame}}\twoheadrightarrow H,\qquad
T_{\mathrm{tame}}=
\langle\sigma,\tau\mid \tau^\sigma=\tau^2\rangle .
\]

One may additionally fix an elementary abelian decoration
\[
\theta:G\to E
\]
and a prescribed scalar-character value \(\psi\). Let
\[
N_\Gamma(G,L;\alpha,\theta,\psi)
\]
be the number of surjections \(\Gamma\twoheadrightarrow G\) whose wild subgroup lands in \(L\), whose induced tame map is \(\alpha\), and which satisfy the decoration.

Then
\[
\boxed{
N_{\Gamma_A}(G,L;\alpha,\theta,\psi)
=
N_{G_{\mathbf Q_2}}(G,L;\alpha,\theta,\psi).
}
\tag{89.1}
\]

The proof is by induction on \(|L|\).

## 1. The two common boundary quotients

Both sources have the same tame quotient:
\[
\Gamma_A/W_A
\cong
G_{\mathbf Q_2}/P_{\mathbf Q_2}
\cong T_{\mathrm{tame}}.
\]

They also have the same maximal pro-\(2\) quotient. In a finite \(2\)-group target, the tame relation forces \(\tau=1\), and \(\omega _2\) acts as \(1\). The wild relation becomes
\[
x_0^2x_1^3x_1^\sigma=1.
\]
Hence
\[
\Gamma_A(2)\cong
\left\langle\sigma,x_0,x_1
\;\middle|\;
x_0^2x_1^3x_1^\sigma=1
\right\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}\text{-}2},
\tag{89.2}
\]
the standard maximal pro-\(2\) quotient of \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}\).

Its scalar cup/Bockstein form, in the basis dual to
\((\sigma,x_0,x_1)\), is
\[
((a,b,c),(a',b',c'))
\longmapsto ac'+ca'+bb'.
\tag{89.3}
\]

Consequently every terminal wild kernel whose chief factors are all scalar is a fiber product of the fixed tame quotient with a finite \(2\)-group, and its fixed-frame count follows from (89.2).

## 2. The first nontrivial tame chief factor

Suppose the wild kernel does not have only scalar chief factors. Choose
\[
S\triangleleft P\triangleleft G,\qquad P/S\simeq V,
\]
where every chief factor below \(S\) is scalar and \(V\) is the first nontrivial simple \(\mathbf F_2\)-module.

Choose \(K\triangleleft G\), \(K\le P\), minimal subject to
\[
KS=P.
\]

Let \(H_2\) be the maximal \(2\)-quotient of \(H\), and put
\[
N=\ker(H\to H_2).
\]
Then \(N\) has odd order. An odd Hall lift \(\widetilde N\) centralizes the scalar run \(S\), by coprime action, but acts nontrivially on \(P/S\simeq V\).

### Critical-subgroup reduction

Use ordinary Thompson criticality in the following form:

> Every finite \(2\)-group \(K\) has a characteristic subgroup \(C\) such that
> \[
> C/Z(C)\ \text{is elementary abelian},\qquad [C,K]\le Z(C),
> \]
> and
> \[
> \ker\bigl(\operatorname{Aut}(K)\to\operatorname{Aut}(C)\bigr)
> \]
> is a \(2\)-group.

Set
\[
D=\Omega _2(C)=\langle c\in C:c^4=1\rangle .
\]
The \(\Omega _2\)-refinement proved in Turn 87 gives
\[
D^4=1,\qquad [D,K]\le Z(D),
\]
and
\[
\ker\bigl(\operatorname{Aut}(K)\to\operatorname{Aut}(D)\bigr)
\quad\text{is a \(2\)-group}.
\tag{89.4}
\]

Since the odd group \(\widetilde N\) acts nontrivially on \(K\), it acts nontrivially on \(D\). Therefore \(D\nleq S\). Because \(D\) is characteristic in \(K\), it is \(G\)-normal; simplicity of \(P/S\) gives \(DS=P\), and minimality of \(K\) forces \(D=K\). Hence
\[
K^4=1,\qquad [K,K]\le Z(K).
\]

It follows that
\[
R:=\Phi(K)=K^2[K,K]
\]
is elementary abelian and central in \(K\). This is the only finite-group structural input beyond routine coprime action. 

Put
\[
M=K/R,\qquad T_0=(K\cap S)/R.
\]
Minimality gives
\[
T_0=\operatorname{rad}_{\mathbf F_2[G/K]}(M),\qquad
M/T_0\simeq V,\qquad
(M^\vee)^{G/K}=0.
\tag{89.5}
\]

Thus \(M\) may be nonsemisimple, but it has simple nontrivial head and no trivial quotient.

## 3. The elementary \(M\)-lift

Fix a generated lower tuple
\[
\rho:\Gamma\twoheadrightarrow G/K.
\]

For every elementary \(G/K\)-module \(A\), the candidate has the finite relation-deformation complex
\[
C_\rho^\bullet(A):
0\longrightarrow A
\longrightarrow A^4
\longrightarrow A^2
\longrightarrow0,
\tag{89.6}
\]
where the final map is the linearization of the tame and wild relation defects.

The finite-Heisenberg replacement makes all first and mixed second derivatives of the \(\omega _2\)-words ordinary finite-word calculations. It yields perfect duality
\[
H_\rho^i(A)\times H_\rho^{2-i}(A^\vee)
\longrightarrow\mathbf F_2
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{Ob}_\rho(A)^\vee\simeq(A^\vee)^{G/K},
\qquad
\dim Z_\rho^1(A)
=
2\dim A+\dim(A^\vee)^{G/K}.
\tag{89.7}
\]
This avoids any asphericity or profinite-\(K(\pi,1)\) assumption. 

For \(A=M\), equation (89.5) gives
\[
\operatorname{Ob}_\rho(M)=0,\qquad
|Z_\rho^1(M)|=2^{2\dim M}.
\tag{89.8}
\]

Local Tate duality gives the same result for \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}\). Hence every lower map lifts through
\[
G/R\longrightarrow G/K
\]
with the same multiplicity on both sides. Nonsurjective lifts have proper image with strictly smaller marked wild kernel and are subtracted using the induction hypothesis.

## 4. The simple-head determinant lemma

Now consider the central elementary kernel
\[
R\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow G/R.
\]

Let
\[
0\ne\lambda\in(R^\vee)^{G/R}.
\]
The scalar pushout
\[
K_\lambda=K/\ker\lambda
\]
is a nonsplit central extension of \(M\) by \(C_2\), represented by a quadratic map
\[
q_\lambda:M\to\mathbf F_2
\]
with polar form \(b_\lambda\).

The following hold:
\[
q_\lambda|_{T_0}=0,\qquad
b_\lambda(T_0,M)=0.
\tag{89.9}
\]

For the polar form, any map \(T_0\to M^\vee\) is zero because \(T_0\) has only trivial composition factors while
\[
\operatorname{soc}(M^\vee)\simeq V^\vee
\]
is nontrivial simple.

For the quadratic restriction, suppose \(q_\lambda|_{T_0}\ne0\). Quotienting by its kernel produces an extension
\[
0\to\mathbf F_2\to\overline M\to V\to0
\]
whose scalar radical vector has quadratic value \(1\). Quadratic invariance eliminates every wild unipotent shear, so \(\overline M\) factors through the tame quotient. But
\[
\operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbf F_2[H]}(V,\mathbf F_2)
\simeq H^1(H,V^\vee)=0,
\]
both in the ramified and unramified cases. The extension splits and produces a nonzero map \(M\to\mathbf F_2\), contradicting (89.5).

Thus \(q_\lambda\) descends to a nonzero invariant quadratic form
\[
\bar q_\lambda:V\to\mathbf F_2.
\]
Its polar radical is a \(G/K\)-submodule of the simple module \(V\), so the polar form is nonsingular.

Every nonzero Fourier pushout is therefore a determinant pushout of the single simple head \(V\); the scalar radical contributes no new restriction invariant.

## 5. The two determinant values

For a split self-dual simple tame module \((V,q)\), let \(S,T\) denote the tame actions and put
\[
U=S^{\omega _2}.
\]

The actual candidate word gives
\[
Q_A(c)=
\begin{cases}
q(c),&T=1,\\[2mm]
q(c)+B(c,U^{-1}c),&V^T=0.
\end{cases}
\tag{89.10}
\]

The Wall-doubling calculation gives
\[
\operatorname{Arf}(Q_A)
=
\operatorname{Arf}(q)+\operatorname{rank}(1+U)
\pmod2.
\tag{89.11}
\]

For a ramified simple tame module, inertia-multiplicity parity makes the two terms in (89.11) equal, so
\[
\operatorname{Arf}(Q_A)=0.
\]
For an unramified nontrivial simple module, \(U=1\) and the norm-trace form has
\[
\operatorname{Arf}(Q_A)=1.
\]

The local Kummer quadratic form has the same values:

- unramified modules give the minus-type norm-trace form;
- in the ramified case, the deep-unit Kummer subspace is half-dimensional and totally singular, so the form is hyperbolic.

Thus
\[
\operatorname{Arf}(Q_A)
=
\operatorname{Arf}(Q_{\mathbf Q_2})
=
\begin{cases}
1,&V\ \text{unramified},\\
0,&V\ \text{ramified}.
\end{cases}
\tag{89.12}
\]


## 6. Fourier reconstruction of the \(R\)-obstruction

Let
\[
B=G/R,\qquad D=(R^\vee)^B,
\]
and let \(X_\Gamma\) be the already-matched fixed-frame surjections
\(\Gamma\twoheadrightarrow B\).

For \(\rho\in X_\Gamma\), write
\[
o_\Gamma(\rho)\in\operatorname{Ob}_\rho(R)
\]
for the full relation obstruction. Finite-Heisenberg duality identifies \(D\) with the complete character group of this obstruction space.

For \(\lambda\in D\), define
\[
m_{\Gamma,\lambda}
=
\#\{\rho\in X_\Gamma:
\langle\lambda,o_\Gamma(\rho)\rangle=0\}.
\]

Fourier inversion gives
\[
\#\{\rho:o_\Gamma(\rho)=0\}
=
\frac1{|D|}
\sum_{\lambda\in D}
\left(2m_{\Gamma,\lambda}-|X_\Gamma|\right).
\tag{89.13}
\]

For \(\lambda=0\), equality follows from the already-matched \(G/R\)-count.

For \(\lambda\ne0\), filter first through \(T_0\).

If the filtration-one edge is nonzero, perfect deformation duality makes it an exact half-split. Moreover,
\[
T_0=\operatorname{rad}(M)\le\Phi(G/R),
\tag{89.14}
\]
by the radical–Frattini lemma. Hence every \(T_0\)-lift of a surjection is still surjective, so imposing generation does not alter the half-half ratio.

If the edge is zero, the scalar class inflates through \(T_0\). Its nonsingular determinant component on \(V\) forces the \(V\)-extension to split by transgression. A marking-preserving shear removes any remaining mixed edge, reducing the problem to the pure determinant form (89.10). Equation (89.12) gives identical zero counts.

Therefore
\[
m_{\Gamma_A,\lambda}
=
m_{G_{\mathbf Q_2},\lambda}
\qquad(\lambda\in D).
\]
Equation (89.13) gives equality of the number of unobstructed lower maps.

Once unobstructed, (89.7) gives the same \(R\)-lift multiplicity. Every lift is surjective: if its image \(J\) maps onto \(G/R\), then
\[
(J\cap K)R=K,
\]
and \(R=\Phi(K)\) forces \(J\cap K=K\), hence \(J=G\).

Scalar decorations impose no additional condition on this block:
\[
\theta(R)=0
\]
because \(R=\Phi(K)\), and
\[
\theta(K/R)=0
\]
because \(\operatorname{Hom}_{G/K}(M,\mathbf F_2)=0\).

This completes the induction and proves (89.1).

## 7. Consequence for every finite target

Sum (89.1) over all tame frames, take the decoration to be trivial, and set
\[
L=O_2(G).
\]
Then
\[
\boxed{
|\operatorname{Sur}(\Gamma_A,G)|
=
|\operatorname{Sur}(G_{\mathbf Q_2},G)|
}
\tag{89.15}
\]
for every finite group \(G\).

Epimorphisms from the cofiltered marked profinite object \(\Gamma_A\) are exactly the generating verifier tuples. Equality (89.15), together with one-sided profinite reconstruction for the four-generated group \(\Gamma_A\), gives
\[
\Gamma_A\cong G_{\mathbf Q_2}.
\]

Thus the displayed block is the resulting proposed profinite presentation. The author’s private 5402-group verifier has still not been executed here; the attached specification makes that run the independent computational check. 

**ESTABLISHED**: Consolidated the entire fixed-tame-frame argument into a standalone proof note. The proof now has a single standard external finite-group input—ordinary Thompson criticality—and explicitly integrates the \(\Omega _2\)-reduction, nonsemisimple elementary lift, simple-head determinant lemma, finite-Heisenberg duality, Fourier reconstruction, surjectivity, and the final all-finite-target consequence.

**GAP**: The private 5402-group verifier remains unrun. A publication version still needs an exact bibliographic citation for the stated ordinary Thompson critical-subgroup theorem and fully typeset standalone proofs of the finite-Heisenberg and Wall/Kummer propositions.

**NEXT**: Submit the unchanged residue block to the official verifier and convert this proof note into a formal manuscript with the three principal auxiliary propositions isolated and cited.

[CONTINUE]
